prompt: As of June 2026, what is the state of the art in VERIFIABLE INFERENCE / proof that a specific model produced a specific output? Cover and rank: zkML (EZKL, zkLLM), opML (optimistic), sampling-based / probabilistic proof-of-inference, and HARDWARE attestation via GPU TEE (NVIDIA H100/H200 confidential computing, Hopper CC, NVTrust), plus startups (Tinfoil, Inference Labs, etc.). Give what is actually practical at LLM scale in 2026 (latency/cost overhead) vs research-only. Cite papers, repos, vendor docs with dates. Then: for a company whose moat is Ed25519-signed evidence ('the artifact is authentic') but that does NOT prove 'this output came from running that artifact', what is the most credible, shippable way in 2026 to extend from signed-artifact to proven-compute, and what should be honestly disclaimed?
content:
**As of mid-2026, hardware attestation via GPU TEEs (particularly NVIDIA Hopper/H100/H200 Confidential Computing) is the most practical approach for verifiable inference at LLM scale, with near-native performance and production deployments. Cryptographic methods like zkML remain largely research-oriented or limited to smaller models, while opML offers a lower-overhead optimistic alternative with economic assumptions. Sampling-based methods are not yet mature for this use case.**[[1]](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/solutions/confidential-computing/)[[2]](https://lightcapai.medium.com/decentralized-ai-systems-cryptographic-infrastructures-verifiable-computation-and-federated-6355d3dea7f9)

### zkML (EZKL, zkLLM, etc.)
Zero-knowledge proofs enable cryptographic verification that a specific model and inputs produced the output without revealing the model or inputs (strongest guarantees, no hardware trust).

- **zkLLM (Sun et al., CCS 2024; arXiv 2404.16109)**: First specialized ZK system for LLMs, with primitives like tlookup (for non-arithmetic ops) and zkAttn (attention). CUDA-accelerated. For a 13B-parameter model (e.g., OPT-13B or Llama-2-13B, seq len ~2048): proving time <15 minutes on GPU, proof size <200 kB, verification 1–3 seconds. Accuracy impact minimal (perplexity increase <0.1 on C4, diminishing at scale). Repo: github.com/jvhs0706/zkllm-ccs2024.[[3]](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3658644.3670334)[[2]](https://lightcapai.medium.com/decentralized-ai-systems-cryptographic-infrastructures-verifiable-computation-and-federated-6355d3dea7f9)
- **EZKL (ZKonduit)**: General ONNX → Halo2 circuit compiler for ZK-SNARK inference. Practical for smaller models (tens of millions of parameters); struggles beyond ~30M params in Halo2-based setups due to memory/circuit size. Optimizations ongoing, but LLM-scale remains challenging.[[4]](https://kudelskisecurity.com/modern-ciso-blog/zkml-verifiable-machine-learning-using-zero-knowledge-proof)[[5]](https://ddkang.substack.com/p/zktorch-open-sourcing-the-first-universal)
- **Other 2025 developments**: zkPyTorch, DeepProve (Lagrange Labs), ZKTorch, zkGPT (USENIX Security 2025) show speedups (e.g., 50–150x or more vs. baselines for certain graphs), but proving large LLMs (8B+) often still takes minutes to hours (e.g., Polyhedra benchmarks cited ~2–4 hours for 8B Llama3 inference on CPU).[[6]](https://blog.icme.io/the-definitive-guide-to-zkml-2025/)[[7]](https://www.usenix.org/conference/usenixsecurity25/presentation/qu-zkgpt)

**Practicality at LLM scale (2026)**: Research/prototype stage for frontier models. Proving overhead is high (minutes–hours per inference, high compute cost), unsuitable for real-time or high-throughput serving. Better for batch/offline or smaller models. Verification is cheap and on-chain friendly. No widespread production LLM deployments reported.

### opML (Optimistic)
Optimistic verification assumes results are correct unless challenged via fraud proofs (interactive dispute game, similar to optimistic rollups). Low overhead; computation runs natively.

- Key works: opML paper (arXiv 2401.17555, 2024) demonstrates 7B Llama on CPU without GPU. Ora (github.com/ora-io/opml) and Gensyn Verde (arXiv 2502.19405) implement it for on-chain/decentralized inference.[[8]](https://arxiv.org/html/2401.17555v1)[[9]](https://equilibrium.co/writing/state-of-verifiable-inference)
- Hybrids like zk-OPML emerging (2026).

**Practicality**: Highly practical for scale—near-native inference speed/cost, supports large models on commodity hardware. Security relies on at least one honest watcher/challenger and economic incentives (slashing). Suitable for DePIN/blockchain contexts; weaker than ZK or TEE for high-stakes use without strong monitoring.

### Sampling-Based / Probabilistic Proof-of-Inference
Limited mature work specific to proving correct model execution. Searches primarily surface sampling for inference-time scaling (e.g., particle filtering/Monte Carlo for better outputs) or membership inference attacks, not verifiable proof systems.[[10]](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.01618v1)[[11]](https://probabilistic-inference-scaling.github.io/) Not a competitive approach for cryptographic or hardware-backed proof-of-inference in 2026; remains research/theoretical.

### Hardware Attestation via GPU TEE (NVIDIA H100/H200 Hopper CC, NVTrust)
NVIDIA Hopper (H100/H200) introduced GPU Confidential Computing (GPU-CC/TEE) with hardware-rooted enclaves, encrypted data paths, secure boot, and remote attestation. First GPU with this capability; general access expanded in 2025.[[12]](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/confidential-computing-on-h100-gpus-for-secure-and-trustworthy-ai/)[[1]](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/solutions/confidential-computing/)

- **Attestation**: NVIDIA Remote Attestation Service (NRAS) + nvtrust tools verify genuine hardware, CC mode, firmware integrity, and workload measurements (e.g., hashes of runtime/model). Supports multi-GPU. Reports include device certs signed by NVIDIA CA.[[13]](https://www.spheron.network/blog/confidential-gpu-computing-nvidia-tee-encrypted-vram/)[[14]](https://phala.com/learn/How-Nvidia-Enable-Confidential-AI)
- **Performance**: Low overhead (reports of <7% for LLM inference). Near-identical to non-CC in many workloads.[[15]](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/nvidia-hopper-architecture)
- **Deployments**: Tinfoil (tinfoil.sh) offers production multi-GPU CC inference on Hopper/Blackwell with open-source stack, hardware attestation, transparency logs, and SOC 2 compliance. Supports open models (Llama, etc.) and private/verifiable workloads. Other efforts (e.g., Red Hat collaborations) focus on confidential containers.[[16]](https://tinfoil.sh/technology)[[17]](https://www.ycombinator.com/companies/tinfoil)

**Practicality**: Most mature and shippable for LLM scale in 2026—low latency/cost overhead, real production use (e.g., Tinfoil APIs/chats). Proves execution environment and (via measurements) loaded artifacts, with hardware root of trust. Limitations include trust in NVIDIA/firmware and potential side-channel risks (mitigated by design).

### Startups
- **Tinfoil**: TEE/CC-focused (NVIDIA GPUs + enclaves); emphasizes verifiable privacy via attestation and open-source stack. Production-ready.[[18]](https://tinfoil.sh/)
- **Inference Labs**: Builds “Proof of Inference” (ZK-oriented) for agents and decentralized settings; raised funding (2025) and works on verifiable workflows (e.g., Sertn). Earlier stage than Tinfoil for large-scale inference.[[19]](https://www.theblock.co/press-releases/360011/inference-labs-raises-6-3m-to-secure-ai-agents-through-verifiable-inference-protocol)

**Overall ranking for practical LLM-scale verifiable inference (2026, balancing guarantees, overhead, and maturity)**: 
1. TEE/hardware attestation (Tinfoil/NVIDIA CC) — shippable, low overhead.
2. opML — practical with assumptions.
3. zkML (zkLLM/specialized) — improving but overhead limits it.
4. Sampling-based — immature.

Citations draw from arXiv papers, ACM/USENIX venues (2024–2025), vendor docs (NVIDIA 2023–2025), and project sites/repos as of mid-2026 crawls.

### Extending from Ed25519-Signed Artifact to Proven-Compute
Your current moat (Ed25519 signature on the artifact/model) proves authenticity/integrity of the model binary/weights but not that it was the one executed for a given output (provider could swap models or tamper post-signing).

**Most credible, shippable extension in 2026**: Deploy the signed model inside a NVIDIA Hopper CC TEE (H100/H200), combined with remote attestation (via NRAS/nvtrust). 
- The enclave loads/verifies the signed artifact (e.g., check Ed25519 signature + hash inside the attested environment).
- Attestation report proves: genuine CC-enabled GPU, firmware integrity, CC mode active, and workload measurements (hash of loaded model/runtime).
- Output the attestation token/report alongside the inference result (tied to the signed artifact).
- Tinfoil-style stacks make this turnkey with open-source components and transparency logs. This bridges signed-artifact trust to proven execution environment with minimal added latency/cost.[[14]](https://phala.com/learn/How-Nvidia-Enable-Confidential-AI)[[16]](https://tinfoil.sh/technology)

Alternatives (less immediately shippable): Layer opML fraud-proof challenges on top, or explore zkML for the forward pass (higher overhead).

**Honest disclaimers**:
- Relies on hardware/firmware trust (NVIDIA root of trust and approved firmware list); attestation does not eliminate all side-channel or implementation risks.
- Proves the attested environment and measurements of loaded code/weights but requires careful integration to bind the exact signed artifact to the specific inference (e.g., via nonce/tied hashes); does not inherently prove every intermediate computation step like full ZK.
- Provider could still attempt malice within attestation limits (mitigated by open-source stack + logs).
- Not a complete substitute for ZK in zero-trust cryptographic settings; best as a strong practical layer atop your existing signatures.
- Regulatory/compliance value is high but depends on auditor acceptance of the attestation scheme.

This combination (signing + TEE attestation) is the shortest path to credible “proven compute” while remaining production-viable.
